question_title: Will Jacksonville have at least 8 days of rain in April 2026?
summary: Jacksonville has 5 rain days through April 10, needing 3 more in the remaining 20 days. Historical base rate is 64% (7/11 years hit >=3 in Apr 11-30), but current severe drought conditions (9.98-inch deficit), a confirmed dry NWS 7-day forecast (no rain through Apr 17), and the pattern from recent dry years (2022, 2024, 2025 all had exactly 2 days in this window) reduce this significantly. The key dynamic is whether Jacksonville remains in drought-pattern (where 0/4 historical analogs hit the threshold) or transitions to a normal pattern (where 7/7 hit it). Weighting 55% drought-continuation gives ~43-45%.

factors.description: Historical base rate: 7/11 years (64%) had >=3 rain days in Apr 11-30 at JAX
Severe ongoing drought (9.98-inch deficit per question description) correlates with dry April patterns
NWS 7-day forecast shows zero precipitation through Apr 17 - first 7 of 20 days essentially dry
Recent dry-year analogs (2017, 2022, 2024, 2025): 0/4 hit the >=3 threshold in Apr 11-30
20 days is substantial time; Florida late-April convective season provides some rain floor even in drought

reasoning:
**Forecast: 0.45 (45% probability of YES)**

Key findings:
- **Current status**: 5 rain days already recorded (Apr 5-8, 10). Need 3 more in 20 remaining days.
- **Historical base rate**: 7/11 years (64%) had ≥3 rain days in Apr 11-30 — normally favorable.
- **Drought headwind**: Jacksonville has a ~10-inch rainfall deficit. In 4 historical dry-year analogs (2017, 2022, 2024, 2025), none achieved 3+ rain days in this window.
- **NWS forecast**: Zero precipitation expected through April 17, eliminating 7 of 20 potential rain days.
- **Late-month potential**: Old Farmer's Almanac predicts thunderstorms Apr 26-30, which could produce 1-3 rain days — but drought years show even late-April storms often don't materialize at the airport station.

The question hinges on whether the drought pattern persists (→ likely NO) or breaks with a normal convective setup (→ likely YES). I estimate ~55% probability the drought continues through April.
resolution_criteria: This question resolves as Yes if the Jacksonville International Airport NWS weather station reports 8 or more days of material amounts of rain in April 2026 according to this resolution source: [https://mesonet.agron.iastate.edu/sites/hist.phtml?month=4\&year=2026\&network=FL\_ASOS\&station=JAX](https://mesonet.agron.iastate.edu/sites/hist.phtml?month=4\&year=2026\&network=FL_ASOS\&station=JAX)
fine_print: For purposes of this question, days with a recording of 0.00 inches, Trace, and M (missing) will all be considered to have had no material rain.
